q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : CeO2 can be used for oxidation of aldehydes and ketones.

Statement II : Aqueous solution of EuSO4 is a strong reducing agent. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : CeO2 can be used for oxidation of aldehydes and ketones.

Statement II : Aqueous solution of EuSO4 is a strong reducing agent. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math> oxidation state of lanthanide is most stable and therefore lanthanide in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math> oxidation state has strong tendency to gain <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> and converted into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and therefore act as strong oxidizing agent. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>g</mi><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Ce</mi><mrow><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math> And therefore <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CeO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is used to oxidized alcohol aldehyde and ketones. Lanthanide in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> oxidation state has strong tendency to loss <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> and converted into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math> oxidation state therefore act as strong reducing agent. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∴</mo><msub><mi>EuSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> act as strong reducing agent.

question: The statement that is INCORRECT about the interstitial compounds is:
solution:
The principal physical and chemical characteristics of interstitial compounds are as follows:

(i) They have high melting points, higher than those of pure metals.

(ii) They are very hard, some borides approach diamond in hardness.

(iii) They retain metallic conductivity.

(iv) They are chemically inert.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reactions

$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CrO}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\text { Vapour })}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}$
The number of terminal ' O ' present in the compound ' C ' is ______.
question:
Consider the following reactions

$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CrO}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\text { Vapour })}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}$
The number of terminal ' O ' present in the compound ' C ' is ______.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2 \\ & \mathrm{CrO}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2(\text { Vapour })+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$

$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}+\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$
No of terminal "O" = 6

subject: Chemistry
q: A paper dipped in a dil. $H_2SO_4$ solution of '$X$' upon treatment with $SO_2$ gas turns into green. The compound '$X$' is :
question: A paper dipped in a dil. $H_2SO_4$ solution of '$X$' upon treatment with $SO_2$ gas turns into green. The compound '$X$' is :
solution:
$SO_2$ gas acts as a reducing agent. When a paper dipped in an acidified solution of potassium dichromate ($K_2Cr_2O_7$) is exposed to $SO_2$ gas, the orange-colored dichromate ion ($Cr_2O_7^{2-}$) is reduced to the green-colored chromium(III) ion ($Cr^{3+}$).

The chemical reaction is:
$K_2Cr_2O_7 + H_2SO_4 + 3SO_2 \rightarrow K_2SO_4 + Cr_2(SO_4)_3 + H_2O$

The formation of chromium(III) sulfate, $Cr_2(SO_4)_3$, is responsible for the green color.

Thus, the compound '$X$' is $K_2Cr_2O_7$.

Answer: $K_2Cr_2O_7$

question:
Given

ECr3+/Cro=-0.74 V;  EMnO4-/Mn2+o=1.51V

ECr2O72-/Cr3+o=1.33 V;   ECl2|Cl-o=1.36V

Based on the data given above, strongest oxidising agent will be:
solution:
Strongest oxidising agent has the highest tendency to get reduce therefore will have the highest standard reduction potential. So among the given oxidising agents the species have highest value of reduction potential is:
EMnO4-/Mn2+o=1.51V

q:
The $d$-electron configurations of $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ are $d^4, d^5, d^6$ and $d^7$ respectively. Which one of the following will exhibit the lowest paramagnetic behaviour?
(Atomic no. $\mathrm{Cr}=24, \mathrm{Mn}=25, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Co}=27$ ).
question:
The $d$-electron configurations of $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ are $d^4, d^5, d^6$ and $d^7$ respectively. Which one of the following will exhibit the lowest paramagnetic behaviour?
(Atomic no. $\mathrm{Cr}=24, \mathrm{Mn}=25, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Co}=27$ ).
solution:
$\begin{array}{cc}\text { electronic } & \text { no. of unpaired } \\ \text { configuration } & \text { electrons }\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad$ Since $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ has lowest no. of unpaired electrons hence lowest paramagnetic behaviour is shown by $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Among the following oxides of 3 d elements, the number of mixed oxides are $\_\_\_\_$。 $\mathrm{Ti}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$
question: Among the following oxides of 3 d elements, the number of mixed oxides are $\_\_\_\_$。 $\mathrm{Ti}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$
solution:
Mixed oxides contain the same element in two different oxidation states.
Analyzing each oxide:
Ti₂O₃ contains Ti(+3) only - simple oxide.
V₂O₄ contains V(+4) only - simple oxide.
Cr₂O₃ contains Cr(+3) only - simple oxide.
Mn₃O₄ can be written as MnO·Mn₂O₃, containing both Mn(+2) and Mn(+3) - mixed oxide.
Fe₃O₄ can be written as FeO·Fe₂O₃, containing both Fe(+2) and Fe(+3) - mixed oxide.
Fe₂O₃ contains Fe(+3) only - simple oxide.
Co₃O₄ can be written as CoO·Co₂O₃, containing both Co(+2) and Co(+3) - mixed oxide.
Therefore, three mixed oxides are present: Mn₃O₄, Fe₃O₄, and Co₃O₄.

q:
A first row transition metal with highest enthalpy of atomisation, upon reaction with oxygen at high temperature forms oxides of formula \(\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{n}}\) (where \(\mathrm{n}=3,4\), 5). The 'spin-only' magnetic moment value of the amphoteric oxide from the above oxides is ______ \(\mathrm{BM}\) (near integer)
(Given atomic number: \(\mathrm{Sc}: 21, \mathrm{Ti}: 22, \mathrm{~V}: 23, \mathrm{Cr}: 24, \mathrm{Mn}: 25, \mathrm{Fe}: 26, \mathrm{Co}: 27\), \(\mathrm{Ni}: 28, \mathrm{Cu}: 29, \mathrm{Zn}: 30\))
question:
A first row transition metal with highest enthalpy of atomisation, upon reaction with oxygen at high temperature forms oxides of formula \(\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{n}}\) (where \(\mathrm{n}=3,4\), 5). The 'spin-only' magnetic moment value of the amphoteric oxide from the above oxides is ______ \(\mathrm{BM}\) (near integer)
(Given atomic number: \(\mathrm{Sc}: 21, \mathrm{Ti}: 22, \mathrm{~V}: 23, \mathrm{Cr}: 24, \mathrm{Mn}: 25, \mathrm{Fe}: 26, \mathrm{Co}: 27\), \(\mathrm{Ni}: 28, \mathrm{Cu}: 29, \mathrm{Zn}: 30\))
solution:
'$\mathrm{V}$' has highest enthalpy of atomisation ( $515 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) among first row transition elements. $\mathrm{V}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$
Here ' $\mathrm{V}$ ' is in +5 oxidation state $\mathrm{V}^{+5} \Rightarrow 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 2 \mathrm{p}^6 3 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{p}^6 \text { (no unpaired electrons) }$

subject: Chemistry
q: The metal ion (in gaseous state) with lowest spin-only magnetic moment value is
question: The metal ion (in gaseous state) with lowest spin-only magnetic moment value is
solution:
Ion Electronic configuration No. of unpaired electron
1 Cr2+ 3d4 4
2 V2+ 3d3 3
3 Fe2+ 3d6 4
4 Ni2+ 3d8 2

Magnetic moment(spin only) is calculated by the formula μ= n(n+2) BM.

Magnetic moment (spin only)is directly proportional to the number of unpaired electrons.

Hence, less is the number of unpaired electrons less is the magnetic moment (spin only).

As Ni2+ has the least number of unpaired electrons it has the least magnetic moment (spin only) among the given options.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The 'Spin only’ Magnetic moment for [Ni(NH3)6] 2+ is______× 10–1 BM. (given = Atomic number of Ni : 28)

Round off your answer to the nearest integer.
question:
The 'Spin only’ Magnetic moment for [Ni(NH3)6] 2+ is______× 10–1 BM. (given = Atomic number of Ni : 28)

Round off your answer to the nearest integer.
solution:
In the presence of ligand NH3, pairing does not occur in   Ni2+ because pairing of electrons cannot create two vacant d-orbitals. Hence, nickel ion undergoes sp3d2 hybridisation and it has two unpaired electrons.

Ni2+=3d8

No. of unpaired electron =2

The spin only magnetic moment 

μ=nn+2=8=2.82 BM

=28.2×10-1 BM

x=28

Cr is expected to have the highest second ionization enthalpy as after removal of 4s electrons, the electrons will be removed from d-orbital. Cr+ have stable half-filled d-orbital with maximum spin multiplicity.

Option -3  Fe+2Ar3 d6,Co+Ar3 d74 s1

subject: Chemistry
q:
The set of correct statements is:

(i) Manganese exhibits +7 oxidation state in its oxide.

(ii) Ruthenium and Osmium exhibit +8 oxidation in their oxides.

(iii) Sc shows +4 oxidation state which is oxidizing in nature.

(iv) Cr shows oxidising nature in +6 oxidation state.
question:
The set of correct statements is:

(i) Manganese exhibits +7 oxidation state in its oxide.

(ii) Ruthenium and Osmium exhibit +8 oxidation in their oxides.

(iii) Sc shows +4 oxidation state which is oxidizing in nature.

(iv) Cr shows oxidising nature in +6 oxidation state.
solution:
(i) The oxidation state of Mn in oxide (Mn2O7) is:

Let us consider Mn as x.

⇒ 2x + 7(-2) = 0

⇒ 2x + (-14) = 0

⇒ 2x -14 = 0

⇒ 2x = 14

⇒x = 7

(ii) The oxidation state of Ruthenium in oxide (RuO4) is:

Consider Ru as x.

⇒ x + 4(-2) = 0

⇒ x - 8 =0

⇒ x = 8

The oxidation state of Osmium in oxide (OsO4) is:

Consider Os as x.

⇒ x + 4(-2) = 0

⇒ x - 8 =0

⇒ x =  8  

Hence, Ruthenium and Osmium has +7 oxidation state in its oxide.

(iii) Sc has ns2 (n-1)d1 electronic configuration.

It utilizes two electrons from its ns sub-shell then its oxidation state = +2. When it utilizes both the electrons then its oxidation state = +3.

(iv) In the modern periodic table, chromium metal is present in group 6. It is a transition metal and belongs to the first transition series. The atomic number of chromium is 24. Its electronic configuration is [Ar] 3d5 4s1. 

It has five electrons in the 3d sub-shell and one electron in the 4s sub-shell. Thus, chromium metal has six valence electrons. Chromium can lose some or all of its valence electrons to form ions with different oxidation states. Thus, chromium shows the oxidation states of +1, +2, +3, +4, +5, and +6 respectively.
The most common oxidation states of chromium are +2, +3, and +6.

So, the statement (i), (ii) and (iv) are correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: When a small amount of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ is added to concentrated $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$, a green oily compound is obtained which is highly explosive in nature. Compound may be :
question: When a small amount of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ is added to concentrated $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$, a green oily compound is obtained which is highly explosive in nature. Compound may be :
solution:
$\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ to form $\mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ which is highly explosive substance.
$$
2 \mathrm{KMnO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \underset{\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}{\longrightarrow}+\mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The spin-only magnetic moment value of the compound with strongest oxidizing ability among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MnF</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>MnF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MnF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> (nearest integer)
question: The spin-only magnetic moment value of the compound with strongest oxidizing ability among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MnF</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>MnF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MnF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> (nearest integer)
solution:
The change from Mn3+ to Mn2+ results in the half-filled (d5 ) configuration which has extra stability.So, MnF3 has most oxidising tendency.

Hence, MnF3 has the strongest oxidizing ability as it undergoes reduction readily.

Spin only magnetic moment is calculated using the following formula

μ=nn+2B.M

μ=44+2=24=4.89=5 B.M

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of statement(s) correct from the following for Copper is/are

(A) CuII complexes are always paramagnetic

(B) CuI complexes are generally colourless

(C) CuI is easily oxidized

(D) In Fehling solution, the active reagent has CuI
question:
The number of statement(s) correct from the following for Copper is/are

(A) CuII complexes are always paramagnetic

(B) CuI complexes are generally colourless

(C) CuI is easily oxidized

(D) In Fehling solution, the active reagent has CuI
solution:
Cu(I) have fulfilled d-subshell and does not have any unpaired electrons so, its complexes will be colourless mostly. and it will be diamagnetic. But Cu(II) has one unpaired electron, so, it will be paramagnetic. 

A,B,C are correct and D is incorrect because Fehling solution has CuII
